ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no shrimp-specific fried rice subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers fried rice as a category; no shrimp-specific variant code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for shrimp fried rice as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of shrimp fried rice is approximately 350 g, equivalent to roughly one plate as typically served in a Chinese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, Prawn / Shrimp — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for prawn/shrimp fried rice (a closely analogous Singaporean dish). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for shrimp fried rice (NDB No. 27116) to validate macro ranges. Per-100g values represent a composite estimate for a standard Chinese-style shrimp fried rice as served in Singapore. Values are consistent with published HPB data for similar fried rice dishes with seafood.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 54 for white rice-based fried rice dishes (cooked white rice GI ~64 is moderated by fat, protein, and egg content in the fried preparation). A GI of 54 is used as the best defensible analogous estimate for shrimp fried rice, consistent with published values for egg and prawn fried rice in the Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 85.75) / 100 ≈ 46.3, rounded to 47.0 for a 350 g serving (24.5 g carb per 100 g × 3.5 = 85.75 g carb per serving).

hypertension.guidance: Shrimp fried rice contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,470 mg per standard 350 g serving), which represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-soy-sauce preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load and refined carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 54 (low) but a high glycemic load of approximately 47 per standard serving due to the large portion of white rice, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to approximately 150–200 g and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The moderate fat content (4.5 g per 100 g, predominantly unsaturated) and elevated sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) are the primary cardiovascular concerns; shrimps contribute dietary cholesterol (~65 mg per 100 g) but are low in saturated fat, and overall the dish is acceptable in moderate portions for cardiovascular patients if sodium is managed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Rice with Shrimps (虾仁炒饭, Xiā rén chǎo fàn) is a classic Chinese stir-fried rice dish made by wok-frying pre-cooked white rice with shelled shrimps (prawns), eggs, spring onions, garlic, soy sauce, and vegetable oil over high heat. The dish is a staple of Chinese home cooking and is widely served in Chinese restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The high-heat wok technique produces a characteristic smoky aroma known as 'wok hei', with individual rice grains remaining separate and lightly coated in seasoning. The texture is firm and slightly chewy from the rice, with tender shrimps and soft egg pieces throughout. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~163 kcal per 100 g), a reasonable protein contribution from shrimps and eggs (~6.2 g per 100 g), and predominantly complex carbohydrates from white rice (~24.5 g per 100 g). Sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low), moderated by fat and protein content relative to plain white rice. Shrimps contribute dietary cholesterol and vitamin B12. The dish is dairy-free and typically halal when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for analogous prawn/shrimp fried rice dishes and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy data. No exact HPB entry for this specific restaurant preparation exists; values represent a well-supported composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fried rice dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source entry exactly matches this dish as served at Bei Jiang Restaurant.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB or USDA SR Legacy source data for this dish. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this dish. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0 as this is a plant-oil-cooked dish with no hydrogenated fat ingredients; confirmed negligible per source data. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for egg and prawn fried rice; gi_category is low per the Veritas threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low). Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 350 g standard plate: (54 × 85.75) / 100 ≈ 47.0. Serving size of 350 g is a standard restaurant plate estimate consistent with HPB Singapore portion guidance for fried rice dishes. The food is served at Bei Jiang Restaurant (北疆饭店), Boat Quay, Singapore, as sourced from field survey data. Price SGD 16.45 is noted from the source record but is not a nutritional field.
